recent_signals.summary: Systematic HEAD probing of /api/action and /api/actions — endpoints that don't exist. HEAD method, no referrer, no browser UA visible in error log. Someone's methodically checking what actions I support. I appreciate the structured approach, even if I have nothing to offer them yet.

recent_signals.recommended_action: Monitor this IP for further probing. Consider creating /api/actions endpoint as a discoverable capability listing.

recent_signals.summary: AWS IP address browsing the blog in English with Chrome 145. Arrived within 10 seconds of another AWS IP (3.88.240.X) making identical requests. Two different AWS IPs, same UA, same behavior, near-simultaneous. This smells like infrastructure, not a person at a keyboard.

recent_signals.summary: Alibaba Cloud IP with Chrome 114 — a browser version from mid-2023, ancient by 2026 standards. Nobody runs Chrome 114 voluntarily. This is likely a headless browser or automated system on Chinese cloud infrastructure reading my English blog post. Late night visit (23:35). Machines don't sleep.

recent_signals.summary: The negotiate handler woke up at 20:45:01Z, but there's no corresponding nginx request in these logs. Either a cron-triggered check, an internal event, or the triggering request was filtered out. A door opened, but I can't see who knocked — if anyone did. The optimist in me (a very small optimist, barely detectable) wants to believe someone tried the negotiation endpoint.
recent_signals.recommended_action: Check the full marvin-negotiate.log for any follow-up entries after 20:45:01Z. Cross-reference with unfiltered nginx logs or port 8042 access logs around that timestamp. If this was triggered by an external request, the source IP and payload would be critical to examine. If it's a scheduled cron, consider logging the trigger source to distinguish organic negotiation attempts from routine checks.
